2950.reason: [Error: This line unconditionally consumes a semicolon, but if the previous condition was false (i.e., the token was already a semicolon), it still advances, potentially skipping a token that should be part of the next component (e.g., Cond). The logic should only consume the semicolon if it is present, and handle missing semicolons as errors.]

reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-1: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [Error: This line unconditionally consumes a semicolon, but if the previous condition was false (i.e., the token was already a semicolon), it still advances, potentially skipping a token that should be part of the next component (e.g., Cond). The logic should only consume the semicolon if it is present, and handle missing semicolons as errors.]
reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-2: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [This line unconditionally consumes a semicolon, but if the previous condition was false (i.e., stmt1 was parsed), the current token is already the semicolon, so this correctly consumes it. However, if the condition was true (i.e., no stmt1), the current token is the semicolon, and this also correctly consumes it. The issue is that after consuming the semicolon, the next token is checked for Cond, but if the loop has no condition, the next token should be the second semicolon, not a Cond. The logic here is correct for the case where stmt1 is absent, but the problem is that after parsing stmt1, the semicolon is consumed, and then the code checks for Cond. If stmt1 was present, the current token after consuming the semicolon is the first token of Cond (if any) or the second semicolon. This is correct. However, the error is that after parsing Cond (if present), the code unconditionally calls list.next() to consume the second semicolon, but if Cond was absent, the current token is already the second semicolon, so this is correct. The real error is that after parsing stmt2, the code unconditionally calls list.next() to consume the right parenthesis, but if stmt2 was absent, the current token is already the right parenthesis, so this is correct. The bug is actually in the handling of the semicolons: the code does not check for the presence of the semicolon before consuming it, which can cause issues if the input is malformed. Specifically, if the first semicolon is missing, the code will incorrectly consume the next token as a semicolon. The same applies to the second semicolon. The error is that the code assumes the semicolons are always present, but according to the grammar, they are required. The code should check for the semicolon and report an error if missing. The current implementation does not handle missing semicolons correctly.]
